quality.rationale: 相对论连续介质应力能张量计算，结合密度洛伦兹变换、薄环δ函数建模与坐标系转换，逻辑链条完整且物理背景清晰

origin.question: Problem 5.1. Calculate the nonzero components in an inertial frame S of the stress-energy tensor for the following systems:\n(b) A ring of $\mathbf{N}$ similar particles of mass $m$ rotating counterclockwise in the $x-y$ plane about some point fixed in $S$ at a radius $a$ and angular velocity $\omega$ . (The width of the ring is much less than $a$ .) Do not include the stress-energy of whatever forces keep them in orbit. Assume $\mathbf{N}$ is large enough that one can treat the particles as being continuously distributed.
origin.answer: $\\mathrm {T}^{\\mu \\nu} = \\frac {\\gamma \\mathrm {N m} \\delta ([ \\mathrm {x}^{2} + \\mathrm {y}^{2} ]^{\\frac {1}{2}} - \\mathrm {a}) \\delta (\\mathbf {z})}{2 \\pi \\mathrm {a}} \\left[ \\begin{array}{c c c} 1 & - \\beta \\sin \\phi & \\beta \\cos \\phi \\\\ & \\beta^{2} \\sin^{2} \\phi & - \\beta^{2} \\sin \\phi \\cos \\phi \\\\ & & \\beta^{2} \\cos^{2} \\phi \\\\ (\\text {s y m m e t r i c}) & & 0 \\end{array} \\right]$
origin.solution:
(b) Let the circle of motion be in the $x - y$ plane. Consider a continuum of rest mass density $ho_0$ at the point $x = 0$ , $y = a$ moving with velocity $ eta = \omega a$ . From part (a), at $x = 0$ , $y = a$ we have $T^{00} = ho_0 \gamma^2$ etc. Now if we consider a ring of matter, all points on the ring are equivalent so that in polar coordinates the nonzero components of $T^{\mu \nu}$ are
$$
\mathbf {T}^{0 0} = \rho_{0} \gamma^{2}, \quad \mathbf {T}^{0 \hat {\phi}} = \rho_{0} \gamma^{2} \beta = \mathbf {T}^{\hat {\phi} 0}, \quad \mathbf {T}^{\hat {\phi} \hat {\phi}} = \rho_{0} \gamma^{2} \beta^{2} .
$$
We now must relate $ho_0$ to the collection of particles. If we assume that $\mathbf{N}$ is large enough so that we can view the particles as continuously distributed around the ring, then $ho_0 \propto \delta(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{a}) \delta(z)$ . The total energy of the particles must be $\gamma \mathbf{Nm}$ so
$$
\mathrm {T}^{0 0} 2 \pi \mathrm {r d r d z} = \gamma \mathrm {N m}
$$
and $ho_0 = \mathrm{Nm}\delta (\mathbf {r} - \mathbf {a})\delta (z) / 2\pi \mathrm {ay}$ . The results can now be expressed in cartesian coordinates by making the usual transformation $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf {r}\cos \phi$ , $\mathbf {y} = \mathbf {r}\sin \phi$ :
$$
\mathrm {T}^{\mu \nu} = \frac {\gamma \mathrm {N m} \delta ([ \mathrm {x}^{2} + \mathrm {y}^{2} ]^{\frac {1}{2}} - \mathrm {a}) \delta (\mathbf {z})}{2 \pi \mathrm {a}} \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} 1 & - \beta \sin \phi & \beta \cos \phi \\\\ & \beta^{2} \sin^{2} \phi & - \beta^{2} \sin \phi \cos \phi \\\\ & & \beta^{2} \cos^{2} \phi \\\\ (\text {s y m m e t r i c}) & & 0 \end{array} \right]$
